[CONTEXT]
"The birds" refers to the assorted collection of birds and animals who fell into the Pool of Tears alongside Alice and swam ashore together. As a loose collective party, they appear chiefly in the early chapters—drying off on the bank, running the Caucus-race, and demanding prizes—and reappear briefly amid the croquet game and trial later in the story.

[FACTS]
- The party consisted of birds and animals that had fallen into the Pool of Tears.
- They swam to the shore, with Alice leading the way.
- They assembled on the bank as a queer-looking party, with draggled feathers and fur clinging close.
- The Mouse told them to sit down and listen.
- They ran a race along a course and began running when they liked, stopping after about half an hour.
- The Dodo declared everyone had won and all must have prizes; the whole party crowded round Alice calling out "Prizes!"
- Alice handed round comfits as prizes; the large birds complained they could not taste theirs.
- They begged the Mouse to tell them something more.
- Talk of a bird-eating cat caused a sensation, and the birds moved off on various pretexts.
- A crowd of little animals and birds waited outside the house and made a rush at Alice when she appeared.
- The party appealed to Alice to settle a question, then returned to the croquet game.
- The King pardoned the company generally.

[INFERENCES]
- The party is a transient, loosely organized group rather than a stable community. [conf:中]
- The birds and animals are easily frightened and self-interested, scattering at any threat. [conf:中]
- They tend to defer to or rely on others (Alice, the Mouse, the Dodo) for direction and resolution. [conf:中]
- Their initial bond formed purely from the shared accident of falling into the pool. [conf:高]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The birds act impulsively and collectively, surging as a crowd whenever excitement strikes—rushing for prizes or making a rush at Alice—and scattering just as quickly when alarmed. As a group they are excitable, demanding, and somewhat petulant, complaining about comfits yet eager for prizes and amusement. Toward the work's themes of arbitrary authority and nonsense, they are passive and easily cowed, growing grave and anxious at the Queen's execution threats and accepting the King's blanket pardon. Their characteristic appearance is bedraggled—draggled feathers and clinging fur—and they speak in a confused, clamorous chorus rather than as distinct voices.

[GAPS]
- The exact membership and number of the party is never fully enumerated.
- It is unclear whether they retain any identity or cohesion after dispersing in the early chapters.
- Their individual reactions and motives are subsumed into collective behavior, leaving personalities unknown.
- The relationship between the early pool party and the later croquet/trial crowds is not made explicit.

[CONTEXT]
The Mouse is a talking animal Alice encounters while swimming in the Pool of Tears. It becomes a leading figure among the assorted creatures gathered after the pool, regarded as a person of authority, until it takes offence at Alice and departs before finishing its promised history.

[FACTS]
- The Mouse was found splashing about in the pool, having slipped in like Alice.
- It speaks, replying to Alice in a shrill, passionate voice.
- It declares it hates cats, exclaiming "Our family always hated cats: nasty, low, vulgar things!"
- It is offended when Alice mentions Dinah catching mice, and swims away from her.
- Among the party of birds and creatures, it is treated as a person of authority and takes the centre of the ring.
- It tells the assembled creatures about William the Conqueror, demanding "Sit down, all of you, and listen to me!"
- It promised to tell Alice its history but, after being offended, gets up and walks away without finishing.
- It growls and shakes its head impatiently when Alice calls after it to come back.
- Later, the frightened Mouse splashes through the neighbouring pool.

[INFERENCES]
- The Mouse is easily and repeatedly offended, particularly regarding cats and dogs. [conf:高]
- Alice's mention of her cat Dinah is what drives the Mouse away in both scenes. [conf:中]
- The Mouse is timid or fearful by nature, described as frightened more than once. [conf:中]
- The Mouse holds a grudge and is unlikely to return to finish its story. [conf:中]
- The Mouse may be foreign ("a French mouse"), though this is only Alice's speculation. [conf:低]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The Mouse reacts before reflecting, leaping or swimming away the instant it feels slighted rather than weighing Alice's intentions. It is prickly, proud, and quick to passion—warm enough to offer its history, yet sharp and easily wounded. Toward the work's themes of nonsense and social hierarchy it asserts dignity and authority, bristling at anything it deems an insult or "nonsense." Its characteristic habits are a shrill, passionate voice, growling and head-shaking when displeased, and abrupt physical retreats from conflict.

[GAPS]
- The Mouse's promised "history"—why it hates cats and dogs—is never told.
- Its origin is unconfirmed; the "French mouse" claim is only Alice's guess.

[CONTEXT]
The White Rabbit is an anthropomorphic creature whose hurried passage first lures Alice down the rabbit-hole, recurring throughout her adventures as a nervous, errand-bound figure. He moves from harried servant of the Duchess to herald of the Royal court, where he reads accusations and verses during the trial.

[FACTS]

- He runs close by Alice and pops down a large rabbit-hole.
- He returns splendidly dressed, with white kid gloves in one hand and a large fan in the other.
- He frets aloud about keeping the Duchess waiting and fears she will have him executed.
- He drops the gloves and fan and skurries away when startled.
- He mistakes Alice for his housemaid "Mary Ann" and orders her to fetch gloves and a fan.
- A brass plate bearing the name "W. RABBIT" is engraved on his house.
- He proposes burning the house down and orders pebbles thrown at the window.
- He appears in the royal procession and whispers to Alice that she is under sentence of execution.

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The White Rabbit reacts before he reflects, scurrying, muttering, and startling violently rather than reasoning through a situation. He is nervous, fretful, and status-conscious—deferential and timid before the Duchess and King but brusque and commanding toward those he mistakes as servants. His preoccupations center on punctuality, propriety, and avoiding punishment, embodying the work's theme of arbitrary authority and the fear it instills. entities.canonical_name: to grow to my right size again
entities.note_md:
[CONTEXT]
"To grow to my right size again" is the recurring predicament of Alice's involuntary, frequent changes in body size as she wanders through the wood. It functions as one of her stated goals and a persistent obstacle during her adventures.

[FACTS]
- Alice names returning to her right size as one of her two objectives, the second being to find her way somewhere.
- Alice states she does not keep the same size for ten minutes together.
- Alice remembers having gone through a number of changes during that day.

- The size changes are involuntary and outside Alice's control. [conf:中]
- The instability of her size is linked to her difficulty remembering things, which she mentions in the same breath. [conf:低]
- "My right size" implies a normal or baseline size she regards as correct and wishes to restore. [conf:中]

[CONTEXT]
"The plan to grow and find the garden" is the two-step scheme Alice formulates while wandering in the wood. It articulates her immediate goals: restoring her proper size and reaching the lovely garden she desires to enter.

[FACTS]

aliases: the executions the Queen had ordered
we should all have our heads cut off
entities.note_md:
[CONTEXT]
"Beheading people" is the Queen's signature threat throughout the garden and croquet-ground scenes, expressed in her repeated cry of "Off with his head!" It functions as the dominant instrument of authority and intimidation in this region of the story, casting a shadow of dread over the gardeners, courtiers, and Alice alike.

[FACTS]
- The Queen frequently shouts "Off with his head!" or "Off with her head!" about once a minute. [ch20_p001, ch20_p002, ch21_p001]
- The gardeners fear that if the Queen finds out their error, they will all have their heads cut off. [ch20_p000]
- The inhabitants of this place are described as "dreadfully fond of beheading people." [ch20_p001, ch20_p002]
- The executioner argued that one couldn't cut off a head unless there was a body to cut it off from, and that he had never had to do such a thing before. [ch20_p003]
- Alice felt quite unhappy at the number of executions the Queen had ordered. [ch21_p001]

[INFERENCES]
- Despite the constant threats, the Queen's execution orders are never actually carried out. [conf:中]
- The threat of beheading serves more as an expression of the Queen's temper than as a literal practice. [conf:中]
- The frequency of the threat ("once in a minute") renders it somewhat hollow or routine to those present. [conf:低]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
- (not applicable for non-Agent entities)

[GAPS]

- It is unclear what offenses, if any, trigger the Queen's orders beyond minor errors.

- We are not told how the inhabitants reconcile the threat's frequency with apparent survival.

[CONTEXT]
"The mistake it had made" refers to errors of misidentification or wrong action that surface in two episodes: a creature pointing in a direction without explaining its blunder, and the gardeners' planting of a white rose-tree where a red one was intended. It functions as a recurring narrative motif of mistaken action and its potential consequences.

[FACTS]
- A creature (the White Rabbit) pointed in a direction at once, without trying to explain the mistake it had made.
- The mistake involved being taken for someone else: "He took me for his housemaid."
- In a separate instance, a white rose-tree was planted where a red one ought to have been.
- The white rose was put in "by mistake" by the gardeners (the Two).
- There is concern that "if the Queen was to find it out" there would be consequences.

[INFERENCES]
- The mistakes carry a fear of punishment from authority (the Queen). [conf:中]
- The gardeners attempt to conceal or correct the error rather than confess it. [conf:中]
- "The mistake" is not a single unified event but a label collecting separate misdeeds across episodes. [conf:中]
- The motif underscores a world where small errors provoke disproportionate dread. [conf:低]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
- (not applicable for non-Agent entities)

[GAPS]
- The text does not state how the Queen reacts when (or if) the rose-tree mistake is discovered.
- It is unclear whether the two cited "mistakes" are meant to be linked or are wholly separate incidents.
- The consequences of the White Rabbit's misidentification are not described.
- No information on whether the gardeners' attempted fix succeeds.
